task: I’m trying to sort out a school project and a couple of homework tasks for this week, but I know plans can change at the last minute. Can you help me think through a simple way to keep moving if one part of the day gets interrupted?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — the easiest way is to build in a **backup order** for your tasks so you can switch without starting over.

Try this:

- **Main plan:** do the most important school project task first
- **If interrupted:** move to the next easiest homework task
- **If you lose a lot of time:** do a short “minimum version” of the project
- **End of day:** use a 10–15 minute buffer to catch up

Simple rule:
1. **Big task first**
2. **Small task second**
3. **Use buffer time for whatever got interrupted**

You can also make it even more flexible by writing your tasks like this:

- Project: ___
- Homework 1: ___
- Homework 2: ___
- If interrupted, switch to: ___
- Buffer time: ___

If you want, I can turn this into a **3-step fallback plan** or a **fill-in schedule** for your week.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s basically the right idea. I’d want it a little more specific though, like not just “big task first” because if I get cut off I don’t want to waste time deciding what to do next.

So maybe:
- project main part
- one short homework task I can finish fast
- the part of the project I can shrink if needed
- a backup spot for catching up later

If you can make it into a 3-step fallback plan, that’d be better. Something I can actually use when the day gets messy.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a simple **3-step fallback plan** you can use without having to think much when the day gets messy:

### 3-step fallback plan
1. **Do the project main part first**
   - Work on the most important piece of the school project while you have the most energy.

2. **If you get interrupted, switch to one short homework task**
   - Pick the quickest homework task you can finish fast, so you still make progress.

3. **If you lose too much time, do the “shrunk” version of the project**
   - Instead of trying to finish the whole thing, do the smallest useful part:
   - outline, one paragraph, one problem set section, notes, or rough draft

### Backup spot for later
- **Catch-up time:** ___
- Use this for whatever got interrupted or unfinished

### Super simple rule
- **Main project**
- **Quick homework**
- **Mini project version**
- **Catch-up later**

If you want, I can turn this into a **fill-in template** you can copy fast.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that works. The 3-step thing is clear enough and not overcomplicated. I can actually use that when I get cut off.

- project main part first
- quick homework task next
- shrunk project version if I lose too much time
- catch-up later if needed

That’s good.
